Name: dynamic_geometric_routing
Title: Dynamic Geometric Routing for Robust Machine Learning Under Distributional Shifts
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic geometric routing framework can enhance the robustness of machine learning models against distributional shifts by adaptively adjusting computational resources based on real-time activation geometry, thus maintaining performance without retraining.
Related Work: While current approaches like Spectral Latent Steering focus on optimizing test-time performance through low-rank projections, they do not fully address the need for dynamic adaptability to varying input distributions. Our proposal distinguishes itself by emphasizing real-time adjustments in computational precision and routing based on geometric diagnostics, addressing both robustness and efficiency.
Abstract: In the face of distributional shifts, machine learning models often struggle to maintain performance, leading to a critical need for adaptive frameworks that can dynamically respond to changing conditions. We propose a novel Dynamic Geometric Routing (DGR) framework that utilizes training-free geometric diagnostics to guide real-time adjustments in computational resources. By analyzing local activation covariances and geometric properties, DGR identifies robust subspaces and allocates precision accordingly, ensuring that models remain efficient and accurate under varying input distributions. Our approach eliminates the fragility of static heuristics by grounding its routing decisions in formalized mathematical frameworks, thus providing a transparent, reproducible pathway for enhanced model reliability. We will evaluate DGR across diverse benchmarks, focusing on its ability to maintain accuracy and throughput while adapting to adversarial conditions. This research aims to bridge foundational optimization theory with practical deployment, establishing a new standard for robust machine learning in dynamic environments.
Experiments: 1. Implement the DGR framework on a set of benchmark datasets with known distributional shifts.
2. Compare performance metrics (accuracy, throughput) against static heuristics and traditional methods under controlled distributional shifts.
3. Analyze the impact of geometric diagnostics (covariance condition number, spectral decay rate) on routing decisions and model performance.
4. Conduct ablation studies to isolate the contributions of various components of the DGR framework.
5. Measure computational efficiency, including latency and resource allocation, under various load conditions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. Potential overfitting to specific distributional shifts in the training phase, leading to reduced generalization.
2. Dependence on the quality of geometric diagnostics; inaccuracies may impair routing decisions.
3. Complexity in real-time implementation may challenge practical deployment in certain settings.
